children.name: Alca
children.description: Razorbills — medium-sized auks of the North Atlantic, named for the distinctive laterally compressed bill that is crossed with a white line. The razorbill (tordmule) is the closest living relative of the extinct great auk and shares its stocky, black-and-white coloration and awkward gait on land. In Sweden, razorbills breed on rocky sea cliffs on the west coast, particularly on Stora Karlsö off Gotland and at Nidingen on the Kattegat. Like all auks, razorbills are consummate underwater fliers, using their wings to pursue fish at depth. They are highly colonial breeders, choosing crevices and ledges in cliff faces rather than the open ledges preferred by guillemots.

children.name: Alca torda
children.description: The tordmule — a stout, black-and-white auk of Swedish west coast sea cliffs. Its laterally compressed bill with white cross-line is unique among Swedish seabirds. Breeds colonially on Nidingen and other rocky islands off the Kattegat coast; winters in the North Sea and Atlantic. Lays a single egg in a rock crevice or cave. Like guillemots, razorbills can dive to 120 m in pursuit of fish. Closely related to, and possibly the living sister species of, the extinct great auk.

children.name: Uria
children.description: Guillemots — the most abundant large seabirds in the North Atlantic, nesting in enormous cliff colonies that can contain hundreds of thousands of birds. The common guillemot (sillgrissla) breeds in one of the densest aggregations of any bird: pairs share tiny cliff ledges so tightly that incubating birds are pressed shoulder to shoulder in ranks, exchanging constant mutual preening and loud guttural calls. Guillemots lay a single pear-shaped egg directly on the bare rock — the tapering shape means it rolls in a tight circle rather than off the ledge. Chicks jump from the colony to the sea before they can fly, at around three weeks old, and are tended at sea by the father. Sweden's guillemot colonies, concentrated on limestone stacks off Gotland and the Kattegat archipelago, are among the most important seabird sites in the Baltic region.

children.name: Uria aalge
children.description: The sillgrissla — Sweden's most colonial cliff-nesting seabird, forming massive breeding aggregations on Stora Karlsö off Gotland. Its upright posture and chocolate-brown upperparts distinguish it from the razorbill; 'bridled' forms with white eye-ring and spectacle line occur regularly. Dives to 180 m using wings to fly underwater. The colony at Stora Karlsö, with tens of thousands of birds, is one of the most spectacular wildlife sights in Sweden and is protected as a nature reserve.

children.name: Uria lomvia
children.description: The Arctic counterpart of the Common Guillemot, breeding on cliffs throughout the high Arctic and replacing it in the coldest waters. Distinguished by a shorter, thicker bill with a white stripe on the upper mandible and a more extensive white throat. A rare visitor to Swedish waters in winter.

children.name: Cepphus
children.description: Black guillemots — smaller, rounder auks that differ fundamentally from their relatives in breeding close inshore, in small dispersed groups rather than large colonies, and in remaining in their breeding areas year-round rather than migrating to open ocean. The black guillemot (tobisgrissla) is one of the most characteristic birds of the Swedish outer archipelago, breeding under boulders and in rock crevices on skerry islands throughout the Baltic and Kattegat coasts. In breeding plumage it is entirely jet black with a large white wing patch and vivid scarlet feet and gape — one of the most striking of all Swedish seabirds. Its piping calls are a defining sound of the Swedish summer archipelago. Unlike other auks it dives primarily for bottom-dwelling fish and invertebrates in relatively shallow coastal waters.

children.name: Cepphus grylle
children.description: The tobisgrissla — one of the most familiar and beloved birds of the Swedish summer archipelago. Its black plumage with white oval wing patch and brilliant scarlet legs and bill gape make it unmistakeable. Unlike other auks, it breeds in dispersed pairs rather than large colonies, nesting under boulders and in rock crevices on skerry islands. Year-round resident on the Swedish coast, turning largely white in winter. Dives for blennies, gobies, and sand eels in rocky nearshore habitats.

children.name: Cepphus columba
children.description: The Pacific cousin of the Black Guillemot, breeding along rocky coasts from Alaska to California. It has darker wing linings visible in flight and a chunkier silhouette. Its descending whistled calls echo across Pacific shorelines.

children.name: Cepphus carbo
children.description: A black guillemot of the western Pacific, breeding on rocky islands off the coasts of Russia, Japan, and Korea. It has white eye-rings and a white bill tip, giving a distinctive spectacled appearance in breeding plumage.

children.name: Alle
children.description: Little auk — the smallest North Atlantic auk, barely larger than a starling yet one of the most abundant seabirds in the world, with breeding colonies in the High Arctic (particularly northwestern Greenland and Svalbard) numbering in the millions. The little auk (alkekung) is a highly pelagic bird, spending its non-breeding months far out in the North Atlantic and Barents Sea where it feeds on copepods and other tiny crustaceans. It very rarely approaches Swedish coasts voluntarily, but in years of severe autumn storms, exhausted and disoriented birds may be blown inland — 'wrecks' of little auks occasionally occur in Sweden when Atlantic gales sweep birds far from their usual pelagic range.

children.name: Alle alle
children.description: The alkekung — the world's most numerous auk, with hundreds of millions breeding on Arctic cliff screes from Svalbard to Greenland. Starling-sized and essentially pelagic outside the breeding season. An occasional visitor to Swedish waters, most often seen from the Kattegat coast in late autumn after Atlantic storms. In major 'wreck' years, exhausted birds may appear far inland at lakes and rivers. Its tiny bill is adapted for scooping up dense concentrations of planktonic copepods.

children.name: Fratercula
children.description: Puffins — the most iconic and instantly recognisable of all the auks, beloved for their clown-like colourful bill and upright posture. The Atlantic puffin (lunnefågel) breeds in burrows excavated in soft coastal turf on offshore islands. In Sweden, the main breeding sites are on Nidingen and Hallands Väderö off the Kattegat coast — the easternmost significant puffin colonies in Europe. Sweden's puffin population is small and has declined sharply since the mid-20th century, making each individual a precious sight. Puffins carry multiple sand eels crosswise in the bill simultaneously (using backward-facing serrations to hold earlier catches while catching more) — some individuals have been recorded carrying over 60 fish in a single bill-load.

children.name: Fratercula arctica
children.description: The Atlantic puffin, also known as the common puffin, is a species of seabird in the auk family. It is the only puffin native to the Atlantic Ocean; two related species, the tufted puffin and the horned puffin, are found in the northeastern Pacific. The Atlantic puffin breeds in Russia, Iceland, Ireland, Britain, Norway, Greenland, Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, and the Faroe Islands, and as far south as Maine in the west and France in the east.

children.name: Fratercula cirrhata
children.description: The largest puffin, distinguished by long golden plumes curving back from each side of the head in breeding plumage and a massive red-and-orange bill. Breeds on offshore islands across the North Pacific from British Columbia to Japan.

children.name: Fratercula corniculata
children.description: A Pacific puffin named for the small fleshy 'horn' projecting above each eye. Its white cheeks and grey face pattern distinguish it from the closely related Atlantic Puffin. Breeds on cliffs and rocky slopes from Alaska to Siberia.

children.name: Brachyramphus
children.description: Murrelets of the North Pacific — small, cryptically patterned auks with a unique nesting habit: they do not breed in colonies but lay eggs singly on the mossy branches of old-growth trees or on remote alpine scree, far from the sea. The Marbled Murrelet's dependence on coastal old-growth forest has made it a flagship species for Pacific Northwest forest conservation.

children.name: Brachyramphus marmoratus
children.description: A small auk that nests high in the canopy of coastal old-growth conifers in the Pacific Northwest, flying inland up to 80 km to reach its nest tree. Its cryptic brown breeding plumage resembles a patch of moss or lichen. IUCN status: Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Brachyramphus perdix
children.description: The Asian counterpart of the Marbled Murrelet, breeding in coastal forests of the Russian Far East and Japan. It was only recognised as a separate species in the 1990s.

children.name: Brachyramphus brevirostris
children.description: A high-latitude murrelet that nests on exposed alpine scree above the treeline, often near glaciers. Its population has declined sharply with glacial retreat and warming seas, making it one of the most climate-sensitive seabirds in the Arctic. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Synthliboramphus
children.description: The ancient murrelets of the North Pacific — small auks that fly from the colony to the open ocean within days of hatching, never returning to land until they breed. They are the most precocial of all seabirds: chicks jump from the nest on the second night after hatching and follow their parents out to sea, swimming and diving from the first moment.

children.name: Synthliboramphus antiquus
children.description: Named for its greyish-white nape that suggests a 'hood' reminiscent of ancient depictions, this small murrelet breeds on islands of the North Pacific. Chicks leave the nest at just two days old, running down to the sea in the dark and swimming out with their parents.

children.name: Synthliboramphus wumizusume
children.description: A rare murrelet breeding only on remote islands off Japan and the Korean Peninsula, with a distinctive white crown patch and a slender dagger-like bill. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Synthliboramphus craveri
children.description: A murrelet of the Gulf of California and Pacific coast of Baja California, breeding on arid desert islands in warm waters unlike any other auk. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Synthliboramphus hypoleucus
children.description: A small black-and-white murrelet of the California Current system, breeding on islands off southern California and Baja California. It has suffered from introduced predators on its breeding islands. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Synthliboramphus scrippsi
children.description: Split from Xantus's Murrelet in 2012, this species has a darker cap and less extensive white cheek than its southern relative. It breeds on islands from southern California to central Baja California.

children.name: Ptychoramphus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Cassin's Auklet — a small, stubby-billed auklet of the North Pacific that is one of the most abundant seabirds on the California Current system. It nests in burrows on offshore islands and is an important indicator of ocean health for fisheries management.

children.name: Ptychoramphus aleuticus
children.description: A small, dark auklet with a distinctive white belly, short bill, and pale eye. It is the most common breeding seabird in the California Current, with millions nesting in burrows on islands from Alaska to Baja California. Its breeding success is closely monitored as an indicator of krill availability.

children.name: Aethia
children.description: The four smallest auklets — tiny seabirds of the North Pacific that breed in immense colonies on volcanic islands, often alongside each other. They are adorned with a remarkable array of facial plumes and horny plates used in courtship displays. The Least Auklet at 85 g is the smallest of all alcids, while the Crested Auklet has a forward-curving forehead crest and produces a strong citrus-like scent during the breeding season.

children.name: Aethia psittacula
children.description: The largest auklet, with a distinctive bright orange-red bill shaped like a parakeet's. It raises a single chick in rock crevices on remote islands of the Bering Sea and North Pacific. More solitary than other auklets, it rarely forms the dense aggregations typical of its congeners.

children.name: Aethia pusilla
children.description: The smallest alcid, weighing just 85 g, yet one of the most abundant seabirds in the world, with colonies in the Bering Sea numbering over a million birds. Its black-and-white plumage and tiny size make it look like a flying puffball. Feeds on copepods and tiny crustaceans.

children.name: Aethia pygmaea
children.description: A small auklet adorned with three distinct white facial plumes that curl forward from the eye, the forehead, and the chin. It is nocturnal at the colony to avoid predation by gulls and has the most restricted breeding range of any alcid, confined largely to the Aleutian Islands.

children.name: Aethia cristatella
children.description: Instantly recognisable by a curved black crest sweeping forward from the forehead. During the breeding season it produces a distinctive citrus-like scent from specialised feathers on the back, used in courtship displays. Breeds in massive, noisy colonies on Bering Sea islands.

children.name: Cerorhinca
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Rhinoceros Auklet — a puffin-like auk that is the closest living relative of the puffins. It is named for the horny 'rhinoceros-like' projection at the base of the bill in breeding season. Despite its name it is larger and heavier than any true auklet, approaching the size of a small puffin.

children.name: Cerorhinca monocerata
children.description: Named for the horn-like fleshy knob at the base of its bill in breeding plumage, this auk breeds on Pacific islands from California to Japan. It nests in burrows that can extend several metres underground and carries fish crosswise in its bill like puffins. The horn is shed in winter, leaving a bare patch.

children.name: Pinguinus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Great Auk — the largest of all auks and the only one entirely flightless, rendered vulnerable by its inability to fly and ruthlessly exterminated by 19th-century hunters for its feathers, meat, and oil. The last breeding pair was killed on Eldey Island, Iceland, in 1844, and the last confirmed individual was seen on the Grand Banks of Newfoundland in 1852. The Great Auk is one of the most iconic species extinctions of the modern era.

children.name: Pinguinus impennis
children.description: A large, flightless auk of the North Atlantic, standing 75–85 cm tall and weighing about 5 kg. Its black back, white belly, and large hooked bill made it superficially penguin-like. It bred on remote rocky islands from Iceland to Newfoundland and was hunted to extinction by 1844.
